editorial.h2: Play with how you look on camera — no shoot required
editorial.lead: Two photos, an outfit choice, a pose, a background. Generate. See what comes back. Some results surprise you. Some you'll keep. 5 free credits to find out.

bullets: Seeing yourself in an outfit before you buy it.
Trying different poses and energies for your social profiles.
Having fun with AI portraits without needing to code or write prompts.
Experimenting with creative looks you'd never try at a real shoot.
Not for e-commerce product shots or guaranteed professional headshots.

buildLook.h2: Build your look from two photos
buildLook.lead: You need one full-body photo and one face close-up. That's the starting point. The app uses them as a reference to keep your likeness consistent across generations.
buildLook.imageAlt: Building a personal look from reference photos for an AI portrait session

bullets: Photo 1: full body, head-to-toe, good lighting.
Photo 2: face close-up, eyes visible, no heavy filters.
Optional: a third photo from a different angle — may help in some cases.
Can't get a clean face shot? The studio can crop one from photo 1.
Formal: suit, tuxedo, jacket — full detail control.
Casual: top, outerwear, bottom — with headwear and accessory options.
Each choice shapes the prompt the AI works from.
The system tries to keep you recognizable — it does not lock your face deterministically.
Better photos = more consistent results. Low light, blurry, or heavy-filtered photos degrade results.
Drift happens. When it does, regenerate or try different settings — that's normal.

photoshoot.h2: Run a shoot: you direct, AI generates
photoshoot.lead: Pick a pose from the library, choose a background, and optionally add items you've uploaded. Each combination is a generation — a few credits, a few seconds.
photoshoot.imageAlt: Directing an AI photoshoot with pose, expression, background, and optional items

bullets: Pose: 100+ options — studio, editorial, seated, dynamic, social, and more.
Background: studio seamless, architectural wall, luxury office, city night, and more.
Expression and camera angle are set per pose, or you can override them.
Items: upload your own (jacket, shoes, accessory, etc.) to layer into the shot.
Most shots take seconds. Results vary — that's built into the experience.
Hands, thin straps, and jewelry are harder for any AI to render cleanly.
Simpler combinations tend to give more consistent results.
Download what works. Skip what doesn't.

blocks.h3: What you can use results for
bullets: Social media profile pictures — some people use them for Instagram or LinkedIn.
Creative experimentation — seeing yourself in a style you'd never try in real life.
Fun — that's genuinely part of the point.
No guarantees on photorealism or identity accuracy — results vary.

experiment.h2: Tune, iterate, and download what you keep
experiment.lead: Not every generation will land. When one is close but not quite right, Tune lets you swap or refine a layer without rebuilding the whole shot.
experiment.imageAlt: Iterating on AI portrait results and Tune edits with garment references

bullets: Select an existing result and open Tune.
Add a text instruction, optionally upload a garment photo (jacket, shoes, top, etc.).
Tune edits the clothing layer while keeping the rest of the shot intact — within model limits.
Tune is enabled by default but can be turned off by configuration.
Each generation costs credits. Tune costs credits too.
You get 5 free credits at signup — no payment needed.
Think in batches: a few modest variations usually beat one perfect prompt attempt.
Small changes (different pose, simpler background) often fix a near-miss.
AI portraits are not photorealistic in a guaranteed sense — results vary widely.
Identity drift is real: some generations will look less like you than others.
Accessories, hands, and fine fabric detail are known weak spots.
We don't store your outputs long-term — download what you want to keep.

faq.intro: Honest answers about what PublicSelf does and doesn't do.

items.a: A web app where you upload two photos of yourself, pick an outfit, pose, and background, and generate an AI portrait. It's for experimenting with how you look on camera — not for guaranteed professional headshots.

items.q: How many photos do I need to upload?
items.a: Two: one full-body photo (head to toe, good lighting) and one face close-up (eyes visible, no heavy filters). A third photo from a different angle is optional and may help in some cases. If you can only get a clear full-body shot, the studio can crop a face reference from it.

items.q: Do I get any free credits?
items.a: Yes — you get 5 free credits when you sign up, no payment required. That's enough to try a couple of generations and see if the experience works for you.

items.q: Will it look exactly like me?
items.a: It tries to. The system uses your reference photos to guide the generation, but it doesn't lock your face deterministically — AI models have limits. Better photos (clear lighting, visible features, no heavy filters) give more consistent results. Some generations will be closer than others.

items.q: Can I use the results on LinkedIn or Instagram?
items.a: Many people do. You're responsible for following each platform's rules, including disclosure where required for AI-generated or AI-assisted content. Results vary — some generations are good enough to post, many won't be.

Tune is an edit pass on an existing result. It lets you swap or refine a clothing layer — often using a garment photo you upload — without regenerating the whole image. It costs credits and works within model limits. Tune is enabled by default but can be turned off.

items.q: What happens when a generation doesn't look right?
items.a: Regenerate with small changes. Try a different pose, a simpler background, or less clutter. Hands, thin straps, and accessories are the most common weak points for AI portrait generators. Iteration is part of the process — not a bug.

items.q: Can I upload any outfit image as a reference?
items.a: You can upload your own items (jackets, shoes, accessories, etc.) and the studio will try to incorporate them. There's no general 'paste any URL' feature — you upload via the item uploader in the studio.

items.q: Is this for professional headshots or e-commerce?
items.a: No. PublicSelf is for personal experimentation with how you look on camera. It's not designed for SKU-accurate product photography or guaranteed professional headshots. Some people get results they use for LinkedIn — that's a bonus, not the promise.

items.q: Do you store my photos or results?
items.a: Your reference photos are used for generation. Results are stored temporarily — we don't promise long-term hosting. Download anything you want to keep.
Credits pay for generation attempts. You start with 5 free credits. Additional packs are available on this page. Exact credit costs per generation depend on the current configuration in the studio.
